content.verdict: Authelia and openarchiver solve unrelated problems in a self-hosted infrastructure stack: Authelia is an open-source authentication and authorization server that sits behind a reverse proxy to provide SSO and MFA for other self-hosted apps, while openarchiver is an open-source email archiving platform that connects to Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, or IMAP (or imports PST/EML files) for backup, compliance, and eDiscovery. Neither is a substitute for the other — one gates who can log in, the other retains and searches email records.
content.bestForToolA: Teams running multiple self-hosted web apps behind a reverse proxy who want centralized SSO and MFA (Passkeys, WebAuthn, OTP, push notifications) without a commercial identity provider.
content.bestForToolB: Organizations that need to archive Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, or IMAP mailboxes, or import legacy PST/EML files, for compliance and eDiscovery with full-text search, on their own infrastructure.
content.whoNeedsBoth: A self-hosted IT environment could deploy Authelia to gate SSO/MFA access to internal admin tools, including openarchiver's own web interface, while running openarchiver behind it to archive company email for compliance.

keyDifferences.title: Core Function
keyDifferences.toolA: Authelia is an authentication and authorization server providing SSO and MFA for self-hosted apps.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver is an email archiving platform for backup, compliance, and eDiscovery with full-text search.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: The two tools address entirely different security and compliance requirements.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Security engineers evaluating access control versus compliance/records teams evaluating retention.

keyDifferences.title: Deployment Model
keyDifferences.toolA: Authelia requires a reverse proxy to function and ships as a container image under 20MB with typical memory usage under 30MB.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver connects directly to mail platforms via connectors and PST/EML import rather than sitting in front of a reverse proxy.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Architecture requirements differ significantly for what has to be in place before deployment.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Infra teams planning what else needs to exist in the stack before rollout.

keyDifferences.title: Licensing & Cost Transparency
keyDifferences.toolA: Authelia is fully free and open source under Apache 2.0, with a single free plan and no paid tier.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver is listed with a Freemium pricing model and described as a free email archiver, but Gappsy's data doesn't document a specific paid tier or starting price for it.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Buyers comparing budgets need clarity on what's actually free versus what unlocks behind payment.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Budget-conscious IT teams comparing a fully free tool against a freemium one with an undocumented paid tier.

keyDifferences.title: Authentication Methods vs Mail Connectors
keyDifferences.toolA: Authelia supports Passkeys, one-time passwords, mobile push notifications, and WebAuthn, and operates as an OpenID Certified provider.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver connects to Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, and generic IMAP servers, plus PST and EML file import for historical archives.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Each tool's usefulness hinges on the specific protocols or platforms it integrates with.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams needing specific modern auth methods versus teams needing specific mailbox platform coverage.

keyDifferences.title: Compliance Role
keyDifferences.toolA: Authelia's compliance value is access governance and audit, enforced through MFA and granular authorization policies.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver is explicitly built for email backup, compliance, and eDiscovery, retaining searchable records rather than governing access.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Only one of the two tools actually retains searchable records for legal or regulatory review.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Legal/compliance teams needing eDiscovery versus security teams needing access governance.

rows.note: Authelia is a companion to a reverse proxy, not standalone

faqs.question: Can Authelia and openarchiver be used together?
faqs.answer: Yes. Since they serve different layers of a self-hosted stack, some teams use Authelia to gate access to admin tools such as openarchiver's web interface, while using openarchiver itself to archive email.

faqs.question: Is openarchiver free like Authelia?
faqs.answer: Authelia is fully free and open source under Apache 2.0 with a single free plan. openarchiver is listed as Freemium and described as a free email archiver, but Gappsy's data doesn't document a specific paid tier or starting price.

faqs.question: Does Authelia archive email?
faqs.answer: No. Authelia is an authentication and authorization server for SSO and MFA; it has no email archiving, connector, or import functionality.

faqs.question: Does openarchiver handle login or SSO for other apps?
faqs.answer: No. openarchiver's documented features are Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, and IMAP connectors plus PST/EML import for archiving; it is not an identity provider.

faqs.question: What authentication methods does Authelia support?
faqs.answer: Passkeys, one-time passwords, mobile push notifications, and WebAuthn, alongside OpenID Connect 1.0 certified provider support.

faqs.question: Which email platforms does openarchiver connect to?
faqs.answer: Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace directly, plus generic IMAP servers and PST/EML file imports for historical archives.

content.verdict: openarchiver and Suricata protect different parts of an organization's infrastructure: openarchiver is a self-hosted email archiving platform for compliance, backup, and eDiscovery, while Suricata is an open-source network intrusion detection and prevention engine that inspects network traffic for threats. They don't compete for the same budget line — one retains records, the other detects and blocks malicious traffic.
content.bestForToolA: Organizations needing to retain and full-text search Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, or IMAP mailboxes for legal, compliance, or eDiscovery requirements.
content.bestForToolB: Network and security teams needing real-time intrusion detection or inline traffic blocking (IPS) using Snort-compatible rulesets such as Emerging Threats Open, particularly on high-throughput, multi-core networks.
content.whoNeedsBoth: A regulated company running its own infrastructure might deploy Suricata to monitor and block malicious network traffic at the perimeter while running openarchiver to retain a searchable, compliant archive of company email — two separate risk domains handled by two purpose-built tools.

keyDifferences.title: Primary Function
keyDifferences.toolA: openarchiver is an email archiving platform for compliance and eDiscovery.
keyDifferences.toolB: Suricata is a network intrusion detection and prevention engine (IDS/IPS).
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: The tools address entirely different threat and compliance surfaces.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Compliance officers versus network security engineers.

keyDifferences.title: Detection / Rule Engine
keyDifferences.toolA: openarchiver has no rule or detection engine; its connectors are for archiving mail, not inspecting traffic.
keyDifferences.toolB: Suricata uses Snort-compatible rule syntax, so rulesets like Emerging Threats Open work without modification, running on a multi-threaded engine built for high-throughput networks.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Suricata's usefulness depends on rule tuning and expertise; openarchiver's depends on mailbox connector setup.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: SOC and network teams evaluating detection capability.

keyDifferences.title: Licensing & Governance
keyDifferences.toolA: openarchiver is listed as Freemium and described as a free email archiver, but its specific license and paid-tier pricing aren't documented in Gappsy's data.
keyDifferences.toolB: Suricata is free under GPLv2 with no licensing fee for the engine, maintained by the nonprofit Open Information Security Foundation (OISF).
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Clarity on licensing and governance affects long-term cost and project stability.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Procurement and security teams doing open-source vendor due diligence.

keyDifferences.title: Deployment Point
keyDifferences.toolA: openarchiver connects to Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, and IMAP servers, or imports PST/EML files.
keyDifferences.toolB: Suricata deploys at the network layer, either inline as an IPS or passively as an IDS/network security monitor.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: The two tools sit at completely different points in the infrastructure.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Infra architects planning where each tool physically sits in the stack.

keyDifferences.title: Community & Governance
keyDifferences.toolA: openarchiver's governance and maintainer details aren't documented in the facts available.
keyDifferences.toolB: Suricata is backed by the nonprofit OISF, with an active community and an annual SuriCon conference.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Foundation-level governance signals long-term project stability.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams doing due diligence before committing to a self-hosted open-source security tool.

rows.feature: No licensing fee for core engine

rows.note: No official managed tier from OISF

faqs.question: Are openarchiver and Suricata competitors?
faqs.answer: No. They're in different categories — email archiving versus network IDS/IPS — and address unrelated parts of a security and compliance program.

faqs.question: Is Suricata free?
faqs.answer: Yes. It is free and open source under GPLv2 with no fee for the engine, maintained by the nonprofit Open Information Security Foundation.

faqs.question: Is openarchiver free?
faqs.answer: It's listed as Freemium and described as a free email archiver, but the exact paid tier and pricing aren't documented in the available data.

faqs.question: Can Suricata archive email?
faqs.answer: No. Suricata inspects network traffic for intrusion detection and prevention; it has no email archiving functionality.

faqs.question: Does openarchiver detect network threats?
faqs.answer: No. Its documented scope is mailbox connectors and PST/EML import for archiving, not network traffic inspection.

faqs.question: What rule format does Suricata use?
faqs.answer: Snort-style rule syntax, so rulesets like Emerging Threats Open work with it out of the box.

content.verdict: Conjur is CyberArk's secrets management and machine-identity platform for securing credentials in cloud-native environments, while openarchiver is an open-source email archiving platform for backup, compliance, and eDiscovery. They occupy different layers of a self-hosted security stack — one secures application and workload credentials, the other retains searchable email records — with no functional overlap.
content.bestForToolA: Cloud-native and DevOps teams needing centralized secrets management with built-in Kubernetes, OpenShift, AWS IAM, and OIDC authenticators, plus a Secretless Broker so applications never handle raw credentials.
content.bestForToolB: Organizations needing compliant, searchable email archives from Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, or IMAP, or that need to import legacy PST/EML files.
content.whoNeedsBoth: A DevOps-heavy organization could use Conjur to manage application and service credentials across its Kubernetes and CI/CD pipelines, while separately running openarchiver to archive corporate email for compliance — two unrelated risk domains that could sit in the same self-hosted security stack.

keyDifferences.title: Core Function
keyDifferences.toolA: Conjur is a secrets management and machine-identity platform.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver is an email archiving platform for compliance and eDiscovery.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: The tools secure entirely different assets: credentials versus email records.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: DevSecOps teams versus compliance/records teams.

keyDifferences.title: Licensing & Backing
keyDifferences.toolA: The Conjur server is licensed under GNU LGPL v3.0 (client libraries under Apache 2.0), backed by CyberArk, now part of Palo Alto Networks, with a commercial license option available.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver is listed as Freemium and described as a free email archiver, but no specific license or paid-tier pricing is documented.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Backing and licensing clarity affect long-term support expectations and budget planning.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Enterprises weighing vendor-backed open source against a less-documented freemium tool.

keyDifferences.title: Toolchain Integrations
keyDifferences.toolA: Conjur integrates with Ansible, Jenkins, Puppet, and Terraform, and offers client SDKs for .NET, Go, Java, Python, and Ruby.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver integrates with Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, and IMAP, plus PST/EML import — no DevOps toolchain integrations are documented.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Each tool's integration surface determines how easily it fits an existing pipeline.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: DevOps engineers automating secrets versus IT teams archiving mail.

keyDifferences.title: Authentication vs Archiving Mechanism
keyDifferences.toolA: Conjur's built-in authenticators (Kubernetes, OpenShift, AWS IAM, OIDC) let workloads fetch secrets without static credentials via the Secretless Broker.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver has no authenticator or secrets mechanism; its connectors pull mail data for retention rather than issuing credentials.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Only one of the two tools manages machine identity and credential issuance.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Platform engineers securing workload-to-service communication.

keyDifferences.title: Governance
keyDifferences.toolA: Conjur is maintained by CyberArk/Palo Alto Networks with active GitHub releases as recent as mid-2026 and over 900 GitHub stars.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver's maintainer and governance details aren't documented in the facts available.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Visible release cadence and backing signal ongoing project maintenance.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams doing due diligence before adopting a self-hosted security tool long-term.

faqs.question: Do Conjur and openarchiver compete?
faqs.answer: No. They're different categories — secrets management/machine identity versus email archiving — and typically wouldn't be evaluated against each other.

faqs.question: Is Conjur free?
faqs.answer: The open-source Conjur server is free under GNU LGPL v3.0; a commercial CyberArk license with enterprise support is also available.

faqs.question: Is openarchiver free?
faqs.answer: It's listed as Freemium and described as a free email archiver, but specific paid-tier pricing isn't documented.

faqs.question: Can Conjur archive email?
faqs.answer: No. Conjur stores secrets and authenticates workloads; it has no email archiving functionality.

faqs.question: Does openarchiver manage secrets or machine identity?
faqs.answer: No. Its documented scope is mailbox connectors and PST/EML import for archiving.

faqs.question: Who maintains Conjur today?
faqs.answer: CyberArk, now part of Palo Alto Networks, continues active open-source development on GitHub.

content.verdict: openarchiver is a self-hosted email archiving platform for backup, compliance, and eDiscovery, while osquery is a free, open-source tool that exposes an operating system as a relational database for SQL-based security monitoring. They serve different purposes within a security and compliance program — one retains mail records, the other queries endpoint and OS state.
content.bestForToolA: Compliance and records teams needing to archive and full-text search Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, or IMAP mailboxes, or import historical PST/EML files.
content.bestForToolB: Security and IT teams wanting to query OS state — running processes, network connections, file hashes — via SQL across Linux, macOS, and Windows fleets.
content.whoNeedsBoth: A security team could run osquery across endpoints to monitor process and file activity for threat detection while running openarchiver separately to retain email records for compliance — endpoint visibility and mail archiving addressing different parts of the same security program.

keyDifferences.title: Core Function
keyDifferences.toolA: openarchiver archives and indexes email for compliance and eDiscovery.
keyDifferences.toolB: osquery exposes operating system and endpoint state for security monitoring.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: The two tools monitor completely different data: mail content versus OS/endpoint state.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Compliance teams versus security/IT operations teams.

keyDifferences.title: Query Model
keyDifferences.toolA: openarchiver offers no query language; it indexes mail for full-text search instead.
keyDifferences.toolB: osquery exposes the OS as a relational database queryable via SQL, with ad hoc querying through osqueryi and continuous scheduled monitoring via osqueryd, backed by 100+ built-in tables.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: osquery requires SQL and systems knowledge to use effectively; openarchiver requires mailbox connector setup instead.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Security engineers comfortable writing SQL queries against endpoint data.

keyDifferences.title: Licensing & Governance
keyDifferences.toolA: openarchiver is listed as Freemium and described as free, but its specific license and governance details aren't documented.
keyDifferences.toolB: osquery is free, dual-licensed under Apache 2.0 and GPL 2.0, and governed under the vendor-neutral Linux Foundation.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Vendor-neutral governance signals a project isn't controlled by a single commercial entity.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Enterprises requiring vendor-neutral open-source tooling for long-term adoption.

keyDifferences.title: Platform Coverage
keyDifferences.toolA: openarchiver connects specifically to Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, and IMAP mail platforms, or imports PST/EML files.
keyDifferences.toolB: osquery runs cross-platform on Linux, macOS, and Windows for general OS instrumentation.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: The two tools cover entirely different surfaces — mailboxes versus endpoints.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: IT teams deciding what asset class they need visibility into first.

keyDifferences.title: Extensibility
keyDifferences.toolA: openarchiver's documented extensibility is limited to its mail connectors and PST/EML import formats.
keyDifferences.toolB: osquery provides Thrift-based APIs for building custom applications and extensions on top of the query engine.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Extensibility determines how easily a tool can be customized for advanced use cases.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Security engineering teams building custom endpoint tooling on top of osquery.

rows.note: osquery moved to the Linux Foundation

faqs.question: Do openarchiver and osquery compete?
faqs.answer: No. They're in different categories — email archiving versus SQL-based OS/endpoint instrumentation.

faqs.question: Is osquery free?
faqs.answer: Yes, dual-licensed under Apache 2.0 and GPL 2.0, with no paid tier from the project itself; fleet management typically requires a separate tool such as Fleet.

faqs.question: Is openarchiver free?
faqs.answer: It's listed as Freemium and described as a free email archiver, but paid-tier pricing isn't documented.

faqs.question: Can osquery archive email?
faqs.answer: No. osquery queries operating system and endpoint state; it has no email archiving functionality.

faqs.question: Does openarchiver run SQL queries against endpoints?
faqs.answer: No. Its documented scope is mailbox connectors and PST/EML import for archiving.

faqs.question: What platforms does osquery support?
faqs.answer: Linux, macOS, and Windows.

content.verdict: openarchiver is an email archiving platform for compliance, backup, and eDiscovery, while TheHive is a collaborative security incident response and case management platform for SOCs, CSIRTs, and CERTs, developed by StrangeBee. They address different needs — records retention versus incident coordination — though both are self-hosted tools that a security-conscious organization might run alongside each other.
content.bestForToolA: Compliance and records teams needing to archive and search Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, or IMAP mailboxes, or import legacy PST/EML files.
content.bestForToolB: SOCs, CSIRTs, and CERTs needing structured case, task, and observable tracking, with Cortex-based enrichment, MISP integration, and 300+ listed third-party integrations.
content.whoNeedsBoth: A security operations team running TheHive to triage alerts and manage incident-response cases might also rely on openarchiver's archived, searchable email as forensic evidence during an investigation — TheHive coordinates the response while openarchiver supplies a compliant record of past communications.

keyDifferences.title: Core Function
keyDifferences.toolA: openarchiver archives email for compliance and eDiscovery.
keyDifferences.toolB: TheHive is a case-management platform for security incident response.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Records retention and active incident coordination are distinct disciplines.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Compliance teams versus SOC/CSIRT analysts.

keyDifferences.title: Free Tier Limits
keyDifferences.toolA: openarchiver's free tier isn't documented with specific limits beyond being Freemium.
keyDifferences.toolB: TheHive's free Community edition is explicitly capped at 2 users and 1 organization, with 1 Cortex server and 1 MISP server; larger teams need paid Gold or Platinum licensing, priced custom per user/org.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Concrete usage caps determine whether the free tier can actually support a real team.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Small security teams evaluating whether TheHive's free tier fits their headcount.

keyDifferences.title: Integrations
keyDifferences.toolA: openarchiver integrates with Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, IMAP, and PST/EML import — no security or threat-intel integrations.
keyDifferences.toolB: TheHive integrates with Cortex for observable enrichment and active response, with MISP threat-intelligence servers, and lists 300+ integrations via StrangeBee.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: TheHive's value depends heavily on its threat-intel and enrichment ecosystem.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: SOCs needing to correlate alerts across many security tools.

keyDifferences.title: Deployment Options
keyDifferences.toolA: openarchiver's deployment options beyond self-hosting aren't documented in the facts available.
keyDifferences.toolB: TheHive offers self-hosted on-premises install, prebuilt cloud images for AWS/Azure, or a fully managed TheHive Cloud Platform SaaS.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: TheHive gives teams a documented spectrum from fully self-hosted to fully managed.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams weighing operational overhead against control.

keyDifferences.title: Governance History
keyDifferences.toolA: openarchiver's governance and contribution model aren't documented.
keyDifferences.toolB: TheHive has been developed exclusively by StrangeBee since 2018; TheHive 4 and 5 no longer accept external code contributions, and the earlier fully open-source versions (3 and 4) were discontinued with repositories archived in 2023.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: A shift away from open contribution affects long-term community trust and roadmap influence.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams that weigh a project's open-source governance model before standardizing on it.

rows.note: TheHive Community: capped at 2 users, 1 org

faqs.question: Do openarchiver and TheHive compete?
faqs.answer: No. openarchiver archives email for compliance while TheHive manages security incident response cases — different categories entirely.

faqs.question: Is TheHive fully free?
faqs.answer: The Community edition is free but capped at 2 users and 1 organization; larger teams need paid Gold or Platinum licensing, priced custom per user/org.

faqs.question: Is openarchiver free?
faqs.answer: It's listed as Freemium and described as a free email archiver, but specific paid-tier pricing isn't documented.

faqs.question: Does TheHive archive email?
faqs.answer: No. It manages security incident cases and integrates with Cortex and MISP for enrichment, not email archiving.

faqs.question: Can openarchiver be used for incident response?
faqs.answer: Not per the documented features — its role is mail archiving, not case management.

faqs.question: Is TheHive still fully open source?
faqs.answer: No. Since StrangeBee took over development in 2018, TheHive 4 and 5 no longer accept external code contributions, and the earlier open-source versions were archived in 2023.

content.verdict: Dependency-Track is an OWASP-maintained, open-source platform that analyzes SBOMs to inventory software components and identify supply-chain vulnerabilities, while openarchiver is an email archiving platform for backup, compliance, and eDiscovery. They address unrelated risk domains — software supply-chain security versus records retention — with no functional overlap.
content.bestForToolA: AppSec and DevSecOps teams needing to inventory software components via CycloneDX SBOMs and continuously match them against vulnerability sources like NVD, GitHub Advisories, Snyk, and OSV.
content.bestForToolB: Compliance, legal, and IT teams needing to archive and search corporate email from Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, or IMAP, or import legacy PST/EML files.
content.whoNeedsBoth: A security-conscious engineering organization might run Dependency-Track to continuously track vulnerabilities in its software supply chain while separately using openarchiver to retain a compliant, searchable archive of company email — two unrelated risk domains handled by two purpose-built, self-hosted tools.

keyDifferences.title: Core Function
keyDifferences.toolA: Dependency-Track inventories software components via SBOMs and analyzes them for vulnerabilities.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver archives email for compliance and eDiscovery.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Software supply-chain security and email records management are separate disciplines.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: AppSec/DevSecOps teams versus compliance/records teams.

keyDifferences.title: Scale & Maintainer
keyDifferences.toolA: Dependency-Track is an OWASP Flagship Project, free under Apache 2.0, used by 20,000+ organizations per the project site, and able to process tens of thousands of SBOMs per hour with version 5.0's horizontal scaling and active/active high availability.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver's scale, license, and maintainer details aren't documented in the facts available.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Project maturity and adoption signal how battle-tested a tool is for production use.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Enterprises evaluating adoption risk of an open-source security tool.

keyDifferences.title: Deployment
keyDifferences.toolA: Dependency-Track deploys via Docker or Docker Compose with a PostgreSQL database for version 5.0 and later.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver connects via Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, and IMAP connectors, or PST/EML import, rather than describing a database-backed deployment stack.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Knowing the deployment footprint helps infra teams plan hosting requirements.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Platform teams provisioning self-hosted infrastructure.

keyDifferences.title: Data Sources
keyDifferences.toolA: Dependency-Track cross-references NVD, GitHub Advisories, Snyk, and OSV for component vulnerabilities.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver's data sources are mailbox platforms and archive file formats (PST/EML), not vulnerability feeds.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: The two tools ingest fundamentally different kinds of data.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Security engineers tracking CVEs versus records managers archiving mail.

keyDifferences.title: Managed Hosting
keyDifferences.toolA: Dependency-Track's cons explicitly note there is no official paid or managed hosting tier from the project itself, requiring self-hosting.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver's paid-tier and hosting details simply aren't documented in Gappsy's data.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Neither tool offers a clearly documented managed hosting path, which affects operational overhead.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams weighing self-hosting effort against managed alternatives.

rows.note: Dependency-Track: none from the project itself

rows.feature: Vulnerability source cross-referencing (NVD, GitHub Advisories, Snyk, OSV)

faqs.question: Do Dependency-Track and openarchiver compete?
faqs.answer: No. They're in different categories — software supply-chain vulnerability management versus email archiving.

faqs.question: Is Dependency-Track free?
faqs.answer: Yes, free and open source under Apache 2.0, maintained as an OWASP Flagship Project.

faqs.question: Is openarchiver free?
faqs.answer: It's listed as Freemium and described as a free email archiver, but specific paid-tier pricing isn't documented.

faqs.question: Does Dependency-Track archive email?
faqs.answer: No. It inventories software components via SBOMs and tracks vulnerabilities, not email.

faqs.question: What SBOM format does Dependency-Track use?
faqs.answer: CycloneDX SBOMs to track libraries, containers, operating systems, firmware, and services.

faqs.question: Does openarchiver track software vulnerabilities?
faqs.answer: No. Its documented scope is mailbox connectors and PST/EML import for archiving.

content.verdict: MISP is a free, open-source threat intelligence platform for collecting, correlating, and sharing indicators of compromise among security teams, while openarchiver is an email archiving platform for backup, compliance, and eDiscovery. They serve different purposes — one shares threat indicators across trusted communities, the other retains searchable email records — with no direct functional overlap.
content.bestForToolA: Incident analysts and security teams building or joining threat-intel sharing communities, exporting indicators to STIX, OpenIOC, or via TAXII push for IDS/SIEM integration.
content.bestForToolB: Compliance and records teams needing to archive and search Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, or IMAP mailboxes, or import legacy PST/EML files.
content.whoNeedsBoth: A SOC could run MISP to collect and share threat indicators across a trusted community while running openarchiver on the side to retain a compliant, searchable email archive — two independent capabilities that could both be part of the same self-hosted security and compliance toolkit.

keyDifferences.title: Core Function
keyDifferences.toolA: MISP collects, correlates, and shares structured threat indicators.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver archives email for compliance and eDiscovery.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Threat-intel sharing and email records management are unrelated disciplines.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: SOC/threat-intel analysts versus compliance/records teams.

keyDifferences.title: Data Model
keyDifferences.toolA: MISP stores threat indicators in a structured format for correlation, exports to STIX and OpenIOC, and supports TAXII push, with MITRE ATT&CK and Galaxy taxonomies for classification.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver stores and indexes email content for full-text search rather than threat indicators.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: The two tools structure and export fundamentally different kinds of data.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Threat-intel teams needing standardized indicator formats.

keyDifferences.title: Community Model
keyDifferences.toolA: MISP supports multi-instance synchronization so organizations can build their own trusted sharing communities, governed by an interlocked contributor license so no single organization can change its open-source model.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver's community and governance model aren't documented.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: MISP's value scales with the size and trust of the community sharing intelligence through it.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Organizations wanting to participate in or run threat-intel sharing communities.

keyDifferences.title: API & Automation
keyDifferences.toolA: MISP offers a REST API and the PyMISP Python library for programmatic access and automation.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver's programmatic access options aren't documented beyond its mailbox connectors.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: API access determines how easily a tool integrates into automated security pipelines.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Security engineers automating threat-intel ingestion and export.

keyDifferences.title: Hosting
keyDifferences.toolA: MISP's cons explicitly state there is no official managed SaaS hosting from the core project itself; commercial hosting and support come from third parties.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver's hosting details aren't documented in the facts available.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Neither tool documents an official managed hosting path from its own maintainers.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams weighing self-hosting effort for open-source security tools.

rows.note: MISP: none from the core project itself

faqs.question: Do MISP and openarchiver compete?
faqs.answer: No. They're in different categories — threat intelligence sharing versus email archiving.

faqs.question: Is MISP free?
faqs.answer: Yes, completely free and open source, with its data format and API also released as open standards.

faqs.question: Is openarchiver free?
faqs.answer: It's listed as Freemium and described as a free email archiver, but specific paid-tier pricing isn't documented.

faqs.question: Does MISP archive email?
faqs.answer: No. MISP is a threat-intelligence platform for collecting and sharing indicators of compromise, not email.

faqs.question: Can I run my own MISP sharing community?
faqs.answer: Yes. MISP supports multi-instance synchronization, allowing organizations to build and operate their own sharing communities.

faqs.question: Does openarchiver share threat intelligence?
faqs.answer: No. Its documented scope is mailbox archiving and import, not indicator sharing.

content.verdict: DefectDojo is an open-source vulnerability management and ASPM platform that centralizes security findings from 200+ scanning tools, while openarchiver is an email archiving platform for backup, compliance, and eDiscovery. They belong to different categories of a security program — vulnerability management versus records retention — with no functional overlap.
content.bestForToolA: AppSec teams needing to import, deduplicate, and manage vulnerability findings across 200+ scanning tools, with RBAC via LDAP, SAML, or OAuth and a REST API with Swagger documentation.
content.bestForToolB: Compliance and records teams needing to archive and search Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, or IMAP mailboxes, or import legacy PST/EML files.
content.whoNeedsBoth: An AppSec-mature organization might run DefectDojo to centralize and triage vulnerability findings from its scanning pipeline while separately running openarchiver to retain a compliant email archive — vulnerability management and records retention are distinct programs that could coexist in the same self-hosted stack.

keyDifferences.title: Core Function
keyDifferences.toolA: DefectDojo imports, deduplicates, and manages vulnerability findings from security scanners.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver archives email for compliance and eDiscovery.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Vulnerability management and email archiving address unrelated risk domains.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: AppSec teams versus compliance/records teams.

keyDifferences.title: Pricing Transparency
keyDifferences.toolA: DefectDojo documents a free open-source edition under BSD-3-Clause at $0, plus a Pro tier starting at $300/month with an automation rules engine, premium parsers, a cloud-hosted option, and MFA.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver is listed as Freemium but has no documented pricing plans or starting price in Gappsy's data.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Clear published pricing versus an undocumented paid tier changes how easily a buyer can budget.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Procurement teams comparing costs across self-hosted security tools.

keyDifferences.title: Integrations
keyDifferences.toolA: DefectDojo imports and deduplicates results from over 200 security scanning tools, with Pro adding integrations like Snyk, SonarQube, and AWS plus a universal CSV/JSON parser.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver integrates with Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, IMAP, and PST/EML import — no security scanner integrations.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: DefectDojo's value scales with the breadth of scanning tools it can ingest.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: AppSec teams running multiple scanning tools that need centralized findings.

keyDifferences.title: Access Control
keyDifferences.toolA: DefectDojo supports RBAC alongside LDAP, SAML, and OAuth authentication, with MFA gated to the Pro tier.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver's access-control and authentication options aren't documented in the facts available.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Enterprise access-control requirements often dictate which tools can be adopted.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Security teams with existing LDAP/SAML identity infrastructure.

keyDifferences.title: Adoption Signals
keyDifferences.toolA: DefectDojo cites 38 million+ downloads and 4,000+ GitHub stars for its open-source project.
keyDifferences.toolB: openarchiver's adoption metrics aren't documented.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Adoption scale can indicate community support and long-term project viability.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams weighing the maturity of an open-source security tool before standardizing on it.

rows.feature: Documented paid tier with public starting price

faqs.question: Do DefectDojo and openarchiver compete?
faqs.answer: No. They're in different categories — vulnerability management versus email archiving.

faqs.question: Is DefectDojo free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the open-source edition is free under BSD-3-Clause; a Pro tier starts around $300/month for additional features like an automation rules engine and cloud hosting.

faqs.question: Is openarchiver free?
faqs.answer: It's listed as Freemium and described as a free email archiver, but specific paid-tier pricing isn't documented.

faqs.question: Does DefectDojo archive email?
faqs.answer: No. It centralizes and manages vulnerability findings from security scanning tools, not email.

faqs.question: How many scanning tools does DefectDojo integrate with?
faqs.answer: Over 200 security scanning tools, with Pro adding further integrations like Snyk, SonarQube, and AWS.

faqs.question: Does openarchiver manage vulnerability findings?
faqs.answer: No. Its documented scope is mailbox connectors and PST/EML import for archiving.

content.verdict: Deskpro is a multi-channel help desk built around AI (chatbot, agent-assist co-pilot, and workflow automations) with flexible cloud, private-cloud, or self-hosted deployment starting at $39/agent/month, while GLPI is an open-source ITSM and asset/CMDB platform that's free to self-host and adds paid GLPI Network Cloud or plugin tiers for managed hosting and advanced features. Deskpro leans toward AI-assisted customer/agent-facing support, while GLPI leans toward IT asset and infrastructure management with ticketing as one part of a broader ITSM suite.
content.bestForToolA: Support teams wanting AI-assisted ticketing (chatbot, agent-assist co-pilot, automated routing) across email, chat, voice, SMS, WhatsApp, and social in one unified inbox, with SOC II, ISO 27001, and HIPAA certifications and flexible deployment down to on-premise.
content.bestForToolB: IT teams needing a free, self-hostable ITSM platform that combines helpdesk ticketing with asset inventory, a CMDB, and agentless cloud inventory, or that want managed cloud hosting via GLPI Network Cloud starting at €19/month per IT agent.
content.whoNeedsBoth: An organization might run GLPI internally for IT asset and CMDB tracking with infrastructure ticketing, while using Deskpro as the external customer-facing help desk with its AI chatbot and omnichannel inbox — GLPI managing internal IT assets, Deskpro handling multi-channel customer support.

keyDifferences.title: Pricing Structure
keyDifferences.toolA: Deskpro's cloud pricing starts at $39/agent/month for Team (minimum 5 agents), rising to $59/agent/month for Professional (minimum 10 agents) and $99/agent/month for Enterprise (minimum 25 agents).
keyDifferences.toolB: GLPI's core is free to self-host; GLPI Network Public Cloud starts at €19/month per IT agent and Private Cloud at €21/month per IT agent (minimum 25 agents), plus self-hosted plugin add-ons from €100/month (Basic) to €1,000/month (Advanced).
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: The entry cost and minimum seat requirements differ significantly between the two.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Small teams or budget-conscious IT departments comparing total cost of ownership.

keyDifferences.title: AI Capabilities
keyDifferences.toolA: Deskpro bundles Deskpro AI — a chatbot, an agent-assist co-pilot that drafts replies and summarizes tickets, and AI workflow automations that route by intent, sentiment, and language — though gated to the Professional plan and above.
keyDifferences.toolB: GLPI's documented features don't include AI capabilities.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: AI-assisted resolution can materially cut agent workload for high-volume support teams.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Support teams wanting to automate first response and ticket triage.

keyDifferences.title: Asset & Infrastructure Management
keyDifferences.toolA: Deskpro's documented features are support/ticketing-focused (unified inbox, self-service help center) without asset or CMDB functionality.
keyDifferences.toolB: GLPI includes a CMDB, asset and inventory management, and agentless cloud inventory scanning as core platform features.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: GLPI covers IT asset lifecycle tracking that a pure help desk tool doesn't address.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: IT operations teams needing infrastructure and asset visibility alongside ticketing.

keyDifferences.title: Deployment Flexibility
keyDifferences.toolA: Deskpro offers cloud, private cloud, self-hosted, on-premise, and sovereign data center options, with Enterprise supporting 22 AWS regions for data residency.
keyDifferences.toolB: GLPI offers a free self-hosted open-source core plus managed GLPI Network Cloud, with a 45-day free trial for the cloud offering.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Deployment flexibility affects data residency and compliance requirements for regulated industries.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Enterprises with strict data-residency or on-premise requirements.

keyDifferences.title: Compliance Certifications
keyDifferences.toolA: Deskpro is SOC II, ISO 27001, and HIPAA certified, with HIPAA-compliant hosting available on the Enterprise plan.
keyDifferences.toolB: GLPI's documented facts don't list specific compliance certifications.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Formal compliance certifications matter for regulated industries like healthcare or finance.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Support teams in regulated industries needing documented compliance attestations.

rows.note: Deskpro offers a free trial, not a free self-hosted tier
Deskpro: $39/agent/mo (min 5 agents); GLPI: €19/mo per IT agent
Deskpro Team: min 5 agents; GLPI Private Cloud: min 25 agents
Deskpro: AI-based routing; GLPI: general IT workflow automation

faqs.question: Which is cheaper, Deskpro or GLPI?
faqs.answer: GLPI's core is free to self-host, with managed cloud starting at €19/month per IT agent. Deskpro starts at $39/agent/month for a minimum of 5 agents, so GLPI is generally the lower-cost entry point, especially self-hosted.

faqs.question: Does GLPI include AI features?
faqs.answer: Not per the documented facts — GLPI's features focus on ticketing, asset management, CMDB, and workflow automation without AI capabilities.

faqs.question: Does Deskpro include asset management or a CMDB?
faqs.answer: No. Deskpro's documented features are support/ticketing-focused (unified inbox, self-service help center, AI tools) without CMDB or asset inventory.

faqs.question: Can either tool be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes. GLPI's open-source core is free to self-host (GPL-3.0), and Deskpro supports self-hosted, private cloud, and on-premise deployment alongside its cloud plans.

faqs.question: Is Deskpro's AI included in every plan?
faqs.answer: No. Deskpro AI (chatbot, agent assist, automations) is gated to the Professional plan and above.

faqs.question: Does GLPI offer a free trial for its cloud plans?
faqs.answer: Yes, a 45-day free trial is available for GLPI's cloud offering.

content.verdict: GLPI is an open-source ITSM platform combining helpdesk ticketing with asset management and a CMDB, free to self-host with paid GLPI Network Cloud or plugin tiers for added capacity, while Zendesk is a paid, AI-driven customer service platform unifying ticketing, live chat, messaging, and voice, with built-in AI agents from the Suite Team plan upward. GLPI leans toward internal IT service management, while Zendesk leans toward customer-facing omnichannel support with AI-assisted resolution.
content.bestForToolA: IT departments wanting a free, self-hostable platform that unifies ticketing with asset and inventory tracking and a CMDB, or that prefer per-agent cloud pricing starting at €19/month.
content.bestForToolB: Customer support teams wanting an omnichannel platform (ticketing, live chat, messaging, voice) with built-in AI agents for automated first response, available from the Suite Team plan at €55/agent/month upward.
content.whoNeedsBoth: An organization could run GLPI internally to manage IT assets, infrastructure, and internal IT tickets while using Zendesk for external, customer-facing omnichannel support with AI agents — GLPI serving internal IT operations and Zendesk serving customer service, two different audiences within the same company.

keyDifferences.title: Pricing Structure
keyDifferences.toolA: GLPI's core is free to self-host, with GLPI Network Public Cloud starting at €19/month per IT agent and self-hosted plugin add-ons from €100/month (Basic) to €1,000/month (Advanced).
keyDifferences.toolB: Zendesk starts at €19/agent/month for the entry Support Team plan, rising to €55/agent/month for Suite Team, €115/agent/month for Suite Professional, and custom pricing for Suite Enterprise + Copilot.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Both have a similar entry price point, but what that price unlocks differs substantially.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Budget-conscious teams comparing what functionality comes at the entry tier.

keyDifferences.title: AI Capabilities
keyDifferences.toolA: GLPI's documented features don't include AI capabilities.
keyDifferences.toolB: Zendesk includes built-in AI agents for automated first-response handling from the Suite Team plan and above, plus separately-priced add-ons (Copilot, Workforce Engagement, Contact Center).
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: AI-assisted resolution can significantly reduce response time and agent workload for customer-facing support.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Customer support teams handling high ticket volumes.

keyDifferences.title: Omnichannel Support
keyDifferences.toolA: GLPI's core support feature is helpdesk and ticket management alongside IT asset and infrastructure tracking, without documented live chat, messaging, or voice channels.
keyDifferences.toolB: Zendesk unifies ticketing, live chat, messaging, and voice in one system across its Suite plans.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Omnichannel coverage determines how many customer touchpoints a single platform can serve.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Customer service teams supporting customers across multiple channels.

keyDifferences.title: IT Asset & Infrastructure Management
keyDifferences.toolA: GLPI includes a CMDB, asset and inventory management, and agentless cloud inventory scanning as core platform features.
keyDifferences.toolB: Zendesk's documented features are customer-support-channel focused without asset or CMDB functionality.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: GLPI covers IT asset lifecycle tracking that a pure customer-support platform doesn't address.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: IT departments needing infrastructure and asset visibility alongside ticketing.

keyDifferences.title: Entry-Tier Limitations
keyDifferences.toolA: GLPI's free self-hosted core includes ticketing, asset management, CMDB, and workflow automation, though advanced plugin tiers (Standard, Advanced) require paid GLPI Network subscriptions starting at €100/month.
keyDifferences.toolB: Zendesk's entry-level Support Team plan lacks the omnichannel and AI capabilities of the Suite tiers, requiring an upgrade to Suite Team (€55/agent/month) or above for full functionality.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Both products gate their most valuable capabilities behind higher tiers, but the specific gates differ.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Buyers who need to budget for the tier that actually includes the features they need.

rows.note: GLPI: €19/mo per IT agent cloud; Zendesk: €19/agent/mo Support Team
GLPI Private Cloud: €21/mo per IT agent (min 25 agents); Zendesk Suite Team: €55/agent/mo

rows.feature: Built-in AI agents for first response

faqs.question: Which is cheaper, GLPI or Zendesk?
faqs.answer: GLPI's core is free to self-host with cloud plans from €19/month per IT agent. Zendesk's entry Support Team plan is also €19/agent/month but lacks omnichannel and AI features found in Suite plans starting at €55/agent/month.

faqs.question: Does GLPI include AI agents like Zendesk?
faqs.answer: Not per the documented facts — GLPI's features focus on ticketing, asset management, CMDB, and workflow automation without AI capabilities, while Zendesk includes built-in AI agents from Suite Team upward.

faqs.question: Does Zendesk include asset management or a CMDB?
faqs.answer: No. Zendesk's documented features are omnichannel support and AI agents, not asset or CMDB tracking.

faqs.question: Can GLPI be self-hosted for free?
faqs.answer: Yes. GLPI's core platform is free and open source (GPL-3.0) to self-host.

faqs.question: Does Zendesk offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, a 14-day free trial is available.

faqs.question: Do all Zendesk plans include AI and omnichannel support?
faqs.answer: No. The entry-level Support Team plan lacks the omnichannel and AI capabilities present in the Suite tiers.
